rank_1_reason: Already effective for the patient’s focal seizures, well‑tolerated, inexpensive, and no pregnancy or hepatic contraindications exist.
rank_2_reason: First‑line, low‑cost sodium‑channel blocker for focal seizures; appropriate if VPA proves inadequate or intolerable.
rank_3_reason: Broad‑spectrum, rapid‑titration agent with a relatively clean interaction profile; useful backup when both VPA and CBZ are unsuitable.

rank_1_reason: Already effective, broad‑spectrum coverage for unknown seizure types, inexpensive and aligns with Ugandan first‑line practice; continue as monotherapy with routine liver function monitoring.
rank_2_reason: Broad‑spectrum, rapid titration, minimal interactions, and well‑tolerated in toddlers; ideal adjunct or alternative if valproate efficacy wanes or side‑effects emerge.
rank_3_reason: Cheap, widely available, effective for many seizure phenotypes; useful as a third‑line add‑on when valproate + levetiracetam do not achieve adequate control, accepting increased sedation.

rank_1_reason: First‑line, inexpensive, and highly effective for generalized tonic‑clonic seizures; no pregnancy or hepatic contraindications in this 5‑year‑old boy.
rank_2_reason: Broad‑spectrum, easy to titrate, well‑tolerated, and serves as a clean alternative if valproate is ineffective or poorly tolerated.
rank_3_reason: Widely available and affordable in Uganda, with proven efficacy for generalized seizures; used as a fallback despite its sedating profile.

rank_1_reason: Proven high efficacy for myoclonic‑astatic (Doose) seizures and already achieving seizure freedom; remains the first‑line backbone despite future teratogenic concerns.
rank_2_reason: Already part of the effective regimen, inexpensive, well‑tolerated, and does not worsen myoclonic seizures; provides additional coverage if any absence component exists.
rank_3_reason: Broad‑spectrum, non‑teratogenic alternative for future VPA tapering; good safety profile and suitable as a backup or adjunct in this resource‑limited setting.

rank_1_reason: First‑line, inexpensive focal‑seizure agent already prescribed; suitable for multifocal epilepsy and likely to maintain the current seizure‑free status if adherence is secured.
rank_2_reason: Broad‑spectrum, rapid titration, different mechanism from CBZ; a viable alternative if carbamazepine intolerance or adherence issues arise.
rank_3_reason: Effective for focal/multifocal seizures, low cost, and serves as a backup when both carbamazepine and levetiracetam are unsuitable.
